new_characters.name: Eldar
new_characters.initial_description: Hadji Murad's young murid — broad-shouldered, woman-thin waist, beautiful ram-like eyes, beginnings of a brown beard; a Lesghian; utterly devoted, silent, statue-like in women's presence.

new_characters.name: Sado
new_characters.initial_description: Chechen kunak, about 40, small beard, long nose, black eyes; master of the house who risks his life to shelter Hadji Murad out of sacred kunak duty.

new_characters.name: Bata
new_characters.initial_description: Sado's brother; sinewy, short-legged, almost black from sun, frayed yellow Circassian coat; cheerful, bold scout who guides Khan Mahoma to Vorontsov.

new_characters.name: Khan Mahoma
new_characters.initial_description: Hadji Murad's murid and chief envoy; rosy, round-faced, lashless black eyes, beaming joy-of-life expression; conducts the opening diplomatic mission to Vorontsov.

new_characters.name: Khanefi
new_characters.initial_description: Avar murid; thick-set, hairy, eyebrows meeting; manages all of Hadji Murad's property and pack animals; sworn brother who manages HM's household.

new_characters.name: Gamzalo
new_characters.initial_description: Chechen murid; short red beard, no eyebrows or eyelashes, blind in one eye, scar across nose and face; silent, menacing guard; instinctively takes defensive position without orders.

new_characters.name: Panov
new_characters.initial_description: Non-commissioned officer at Vozdvizhensk; long-suffering, pragmatic, fair; leads the ambush party; breaks up soldiers' bickering about the cashbox.

new_characters.name: Avdeev
new_characters.initial_description: Russian soldier; cheerful, homesick, went to war voluntarily in place of his brother; forms easy human connection even with Chechen scouts; mortally wounded in the skirmish.

new_characters.name: Nikitin
new_characters.initial_description: Russian soldier; morose, distrustful; grumbles about the company commander's cashbox theft; suspicious of Chechens.

new_characters.name: Bondarenko
new_characters.initial_description: Russian soldier, third man in ambush party; Ukrainian, cheerful voice; accompanies Avdeev to deliver the scouts to the fort.

new_characters.name: Simon Vorontsov
new_characters.initial_description: Prince, Commander of Kurin Regiment, Imperial Aide-de-Camp, son of Commander-in-Chief; long-faced, English-accented Russian; politically ambitious, pleased to secure Hadji Murad's defection before his superior Meller-Zakomelsky can.

new_characters.name: Marya Vasilevna
new_characters.initial_description: Princess, Vorontsov's wife; large-built, black-browed Petersburg beauty; warm, quick-minded, flirtatious with Poltoratsky; immediately charmed by Hadji Murad's blush.

new_characters.name: Poltoratsky
new_characters.initial_description: Company commander, ex-Guard; wide-set kindly eyes; in a state of elation near danger; infatuated with Marya Vasilevna; first Russian to meet Hadji Murad face-to-face; surprised by his childlike smile.

new_characters.name: Baron Freze
new_characters.initial_description: Ex-Guards officer reduced to ranks for duelling; Poltoratsky's fellow student at Cadet College; present at the wood-felling officers' breakfast.

new_characters.name: Tikhonov
new_characters.initial_description: Poltoratsky's subaltern and roommate; brief appearance.

new_characters.name: Vovilo
new_characters.initial_description: Poltoratsky's soldier-servant; brief domestic appearance.

new_characters.name: Sado's son
new_characters.initial_description: Fifteen-year-old boy with brilliant black eyes; runs to fetch his father and then Bata; holds Hadji Murad's horse at the midnight escape.

new_characters.name: Bulka
new_characters.initial_description: Marya Vasilevna's curly-haired six-year-old son from her first marriage; sits on Hadji Murad's knee; symbol of the unexpected human gentleness in the conqueror.

new_characters.name: Regimental Adjutant
new_characters.initial_description: Sits straight, cold handsome face; stern card partner; procedural figure in Vorontsov's household.

new_characters.name: Tutor
new_characters.initial_description: Shaggy young Petersburg University graduate sent to tutor Bulka; gloomy appearance; Vorontsov's card partner.

plot_elements.description: Hadji Murad arrives at Sado's saklya with murid Eldar; Sado reveals Shamil has ordered his capture dead or alive; kunak bond declared.
Hadji Murad dispatches Bata as guide to lead Khan Mahoma through Russian lines to Prince Vorontsov with his surrender offer.
Sado's wife and daughter serve food; silent gendered ritual; Eldar stone-still while women present — cultural texture of Chechen hospitality.
Hadji Murad passes a secret note for his son via Sado — first explicit reference to the family-rescue goal.
Sado knowingly risks death to shelter Hadji Murad; his kunak duty overrides fear of Shamil — dramatizes the moral weight of the kunak code.
Midnight warning: whole aoul knows Hadji Murad is here; Elders assemble in mosque to detain him. Sado wakes him.
Hadji Murad and Eldar make a night escape from the aoul on horseback; aoul men pursue but are bluffed off by raised rifle.
Hadji Murad reaches murids' forest camp; meets Khanefi, Gamzalo; prays; dreams of capturing Shamil and ruling Chechnya — exposes his hidden ambition and longing.
Khan Mahoma returns: Vorontsov promises to receive Hadji Murad well tomorrow at the Shalin glade wood-felling site.
Russian ambush party at Vozdvizhensk: Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin, Bondarenko on night watch; cashbox complaint; Avdeev's homesickness monologue (went to war for his brother).
Bata and Khan Mahoma intercepted by ambush soldiers; Avdeev connects humanly with the scouts; they are delivered safely to the fort.
Vorontsov's midnight drawing room: card game, champagne, Poltoratsky infatuated with Marya Vasilevna; Vorontsov receives envoy news; couple's excitement about Hadji Murad.
Wood-felling morning: Poltoratsky's officers eat and joke about General Sleptsov's death — ironic heroism myth, indifference to actual violence.
Chechen pursuers fire on Russian line; Poltoratsky orders volley; Avdeev is shot in the stomach — mortal wound during a pointless skirmish.
Vorontsov rides up; tells Poltoratsky Hadji Murad is about to surrender; Poltoratsky deploys sharpshooters to escort route.

FORMAL SURRENDER: Hadji Murad and five murids ride out of forest and meet Vorontsov. Hadji Murad speaks his surrender formula: 'I surrender to the Russian Tsar. I wished to do so long ago but Shamil would not let me.' Handshake; retinue introduced.
Soldiers comment on Hadji Murad: mix of admiration and resentment — 'What a lot of men that damned fellow has destroyed!' vs. 'He's a fine fellow — a real dzhigit!'
Hadji Murad brought to Vorontsov's house; Marya Vasilevna receives him; he blushes at her hand; sits with little Bulka on his knee — human tenderness behind the warrior mask.
Vorontsov writes reports to General Kozlovsky and to his father — bureaucratic machinery activates around HM's defection; Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety about Meller-Zakomelsky surfaces.
Kunak-gift exchange conversation: Marya Vasilevna teases HM that he'll go about like Adam; HM explains the kunak gift law. Light humor as political ice-breaker.

new_locations.name: Sado's Saklya (Chechen Aoul)
new_locations.description: Guest chamber of kunak Sado in an unnamed Chechen aoul; low round table, cushions, fireplace niche with night light; women's quarters separate. Moral and narrative core: sacred hospitality space under mortal threat.

new_locations.name: Chechen Aoul Night Streets
new_locations.description: Dark lanes and the upper mosque of the aoul at midnight; narrow sidestreets, wooden-shoe clatter, minarets visible against the stars. Site of Hadji Murad's escape.

new_locations.name: Forest Camp (Murids' Glade)
new_locations.description: Glade in the forest beyond the aoul ravine; bonfire quickly extinguished; hobbled horse; burka on ground. Hadji Murad's hidden command post before the surrender.

new_locations.name: Vozdvizhensk Fort
new_locations.description: Advanced Russian fort ten miles from Sado's aoul; barracks, soldiers' houses, best house (Vorontsov's); the Russian command center and hinge point of the plot.

new_locations.name: Ambush Post (Plane Tree)
new_locations.description: Night outpost 20 paces off the road through forest; blackened broken plane tree; low-stakes watch that becomes the intercept point for Bata and Khan Mahoma.

new_locations.name: Vorontsov Drawing Room
new_locations.description: Spacious carpeted room with rich curtains, four-candle card table, champagne; theatrical imperial domesticity in a frontier fort.

new_locations.name: Shalin Glade (Wood-Felling Site)
new_locations.description: Forest glade six miles beyond Shagirinsk Gate; log-felling operation, bonfires in morning mist; site of the formal surrender meeting — cinematically critical.

new_locations.name: Road to Fort (Surrender Route)
new_locations.description: Forest road from glade back to Vozdvizhensk along which Vorontsov and Hadji Murad ride together after the handshake.

theme_hints: Sacred hospitality vs. political survival (kunak code under Shamil's death order)
The human beneath the warrior: Hadji Murad's blush, his gentleness with Bulka, his childlike smile
Parallel worlds: Chechen kunak night / Russian card-and-champagne night — same stakes, different register
Avdeev as the human cost: the ordinary man who substituted for his brother, now shot for nothing
Defection as double betrayal: HM betrays Shamil, but also risks betraying his own people
Imperial machinery vs. human scale: soldiers' gossip, Vorontsov's jurisdictional anxiety, Marya Vasilevna's drawing room politics
Dreams and reality: HM dreams of capturing Shamil, wakes to jackals — desire vs. dependence
The heroism myth (Sleptsov chapter): soldiers joke about hand-to-hand glory while Avdeev bleeds
symbols: Kunak bond — sacred obligation that overrides self-preservation
Stars and the Pleiades — HM checks them mid-prayer; time passing, fate marking
Rifle raised in darkness — power through threat, not violence; HM's authority without bloodshed
Avdeev's wound in the stomach — the gut-shot of pointless war, the body as price of abstraction
Hadji Murad on white-maned horse with gold ornaments — the legend made visible for the first time
Bulka on HM's knee — innocence as bridge between enemies
Honey at Sado's table — sweetness and calm before the danger
notes: This is the pivotal structural hinge of the entire novella: the surrender. Chapters I–VI deliver setup (Chechen night), complication (aoul escape, envoy mission), parallel subplot (Russian soldiers' night watch), and the climactic Act 1 Turning Point — Hadji Murad formally crosses to the Russian side. Avdeev's wounding here is dramaturgically crucial: it ties the Chechen political drama to a specific human cost at the exact moment of the 'good news' (the surrender). The chunk has exceptional filmability — nearly all scenes are visual action or charged dialogue. The Sado kunak-hospitality section and the surrender itself are cinematic setpieces. Marya Vasilevna and Poltoratsky both appear here fully; their subplot is entirely cuttable in compression. Gamzalo is introduced as a menace — his later role (staying loyal to Shamil's worldview among HM's murids) is seeded here by his refusal to look at Vorontsov.

fusion_candidates.reason: Confirmed by chunk: both are minor crowd-soldiers in the ambush party with near-identical functions (sit, react, comment). Nikitin's grumbling about the cashbox and Bondarenko's cheerful Ukrainian voice are interchangeable — either can absorb the other. The cashbox subplot is cuttable; if cut, Nikitin has zero remaining function and can be folded into Bondarenko or dropped entirely. Confidence 0.90 confirmed.

Panov confirmed as present at Avdeev's wounding. Serogin (Avdeev's other deathbed companion from later chapters) shares identical function of loyal witness. Can be merged in film into a single soldier-friend character. Confidence 0.85 confirmed.

Vovilo appears in this chunk as Poltoratsky's servant — passive, reactive. Ivan Petrovich exists as off-screen reference point for Vovilo's self-pity (later chapter). Both are non-load-bearing. If Poltoratsky's domestic scenes are retained at all, one name suffices. Confidence 0.85 confirmed.

Both appear solely as Poltoratsky's companions in the wood-felling breakfast and bedroom scenes respectively. Neither carries independent narrative weight. In compression, Poltoratsky can speak to one generic comrade. Non-historical, low weight.

cuttable.element: Company cashbox subplot (commander borrowing from funds)
cuttable.reason: Soldiers' conversation about the missing cashbox money is entirely self-contained and non-load-bearing. It characterizes Nikitin's moroseness but adds nothing to the HM trajectory or Avdeev's arc. Confirmed cuttable thread.
Freze appears only in the wood-felling officers' breakfast. Reduced to ranks for duelling — interesting detail, zero narrative function. Cut.
Post-card-game scene between Poltoratsky and Tikhonov is pure filler — characterization that can be achieved through Poltoratsky's visible infatuation during the card game itself.
Confirmed cuttable. His resentment/self-pity arc (comparing himself to Ivan Petrovich) is non-load-bearing. His domestic function can be absorbed silently.

Poltoratsky's romantic distraction during the card game and his reverie afterward are atmospheric but not structurally necessary. Marya Vasilevna's role can be retained minimally (the drawing-room reception of HM) without the infatuation frame.

cuttable.element: General Sleptsov heroism-myth discussion among officers
cuttable.reason: Tolstoy's ironic anti-heroism commentary via the Sleptsov anecdote is a literary digression. Strong thematic content but delivered as authorial voice, not through action. Film can imply the theme through Avdeev's wounding without the officers' meta-commentary.
Confirmed as a cuttable political-framing thread (reach=1). In film, Vorontsov's ambition can be shown through his eagerness to host HM without the bureaucratic sub-anxiety about chain of command.

cuttable.element: Regimental Adjutant and Tutor as card-game characters
cuttable.reason: Both exist solely to populate the card table. Non-historical, zero independent function. The drawing room scene needs only Vorontsov, Marya Vasilevna, and Poltoratsky.

refocalize.scene: Russian ambush party night watch — Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin, Bondarenko smoke, gossip about cashbox, intercept Bata and Khan Mahoma
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage as Bata's POV approaching Russian lines in darkness; the soldiers appear as silhouettes and threat, then as bumbling humans. Preserves Avdeev's humanity as surprise discovery rather than predicate. Alternatively cut the whole night-watch scene and show only the moment Vorontsov receives the envoy (reported in Chapter III).

refocalize.scene: Vorontsov drawing room card game and Poltoratsky's infatuation
refocalize.recommended_pov: If retained: restage as Hadji Murad later being told of the card-game world (e.g. Loris-Melikov describing Tiflis society) so the imperial frivolity is filtered through HM's alien perspective. Or cut entirely — HM's first meeting with Vorontsov at the Shalin glade carries the diplomatic weight without the drawing room prep.

refocalize.scene: Wood-felling officers' breakfast and Sleptsov heroism-myth discussion
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut Sleptsov discussion. Retain Poltoratsky's elation-in-danger as a brief visual beat (him riding up cheerfully) before Avdeev is shot. The irony lands harder if it is action, not commentary.

refocalize.scene: Hadji Murad's forest camp prayer and dream of capturing Shamil
refocalize.recommended_pov: Keep as HM POV — this is one of the rare direct-access moments into his hidden goal and ambition. Film can render via light/shadow on his face, the whistle signal, and a brief dream-image insert. Already protagonist POV — no refocalization needed.

refocalize.scene: Soldiers comment after the surrender procession rides past
refocalize.current_pov: Anonymous Russian soldiers on the road
refocalize.recommended_pov: Refocalize as Hadji Murad overhearing/sensing the soldiers' stares and muttering — his smile in return is already in the text. This keeps the chorus commentary while anchoring it in the protagonist's experience.

refocalize.scene: Poltoratsky's bedroom scene (post-card game, Tikhonov exchange, fantasy of Marya Vasilevna)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Cut. Zero information relevant to HM's trajectory. If the infatuation beat is needed, it is already implicit in the card-game scene.
